author: @Venturestarx
text: Every time I see your videos I smile Robert. That box is much like the flash setup I used for photo lithography of GO. You made it way better, and more practical though. Great job.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @GREENEARTHTECHNOLOGY
text:
Robert,

Thank you so much for your hard work and continuing to make this open source.
Brilliant work.
I agree with the last comment, the Laser printing concept would be the catalyst that would bring us closer to 3d printing.
Have you considered Plasma treating the area for adhesion prior to curing?
Thank you for the EBook as well, I thoroughly enjoyed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cheers mate - I am glad you like the book - I try to do stuff that i think anyone can repeat so - I haven't looked at plasma treating as i think that is a bit beyond the kitchen experimenter - though i may well be wrong not having looked at it lol

author: @YbborNetsrek
text:
Hi Robert. 
Look at this:  Highly-Flexible, Low-Cost, All Stainless Steel Mesh-based Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells
Is states they dye them with carbon ink
 http://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2014/NR/C4NR03999H?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+rss%2FNR+%28RSC+-+Nanoscale+latest+articles%29#!divAbstract

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: brilliant mate - thanks for the heads up on that one - i think i will make one of these as we are just about there anyway - i get a great sense that we are ahead of the game here lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
here is an equally interesting one
http://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2014/NR/C4NR03999H?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed:+rss/NR+%28RSC+-+Nanoscale+latest+articles%29#!divAbstract

replies.author: @YbborNetsrek
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Nice try, but it is the same! ;) 
I look forward to see if you can replicate something like this! 
Do remember: If you happen to find a way to cheap, efficient DIY solar cell..  Some groups won't like it!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Rob NvT
lol - no it isn't one is steel mesh the other is cloth - plus different authors, different journals - the idea is essentially the same - but they are different articles mate

replies.author: @YbborNetsrek
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Hahah well, without wanting to start a fittie.. I believe you, but then you have the links mixed up. Your link is the same as mine..

author: @zeropointfuel
text: Well done, have you considered a laser printer process? I am just curious to hear your thoughts on  a powder type delivery system and once again your videos are always  inspirational.

author: @AaronHarper
text: Can you coat the whole transparency sheet with the Graphene Oxide solution, then mask it using a second transparency sheet with a negative pattern from a laser printer placed on top?  Placed in the flasher unit, the result should be conductive regions defined by the non-printed areas on the mask.  This could potentially be a good way to produce not only flexible displays and force sensitive resistors, but perhaps even ribbon cables, keyboards, or other peripherals and components.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you could most definitely do that and it is a good idea - the stuff in the jar that i show is good enough to go into an inkjet cartridge though - so that is probably the route i will take - but if you can't do that - your resist idea is very cool

author: @vicebi
text: Robert Hola !... como dicen es cool!!!. Creo que la porosidad del papel y le menor del plástico tienen que ver, no oculto que me sorprende como el proceso de Flashing cambia la conductividad del Graphene... mi intuición me lleva a sugerirte y cual será la medición si se usan como base un crital delgado, como los que se utilizan como Portaobjeros en los meicroscopios (microscope slide). Saludos Cordiales Vic... from Mex...
